title: Professional Boundaries in the Nurse–Patient Relationship
subtitle: Over a long admission, a patient offers the nurse an expensive gift, asks for a personal phone number, and wants to connect on social media. The nurse keeps the relationship patient-centered and professional — declining personal contact and avoiding boundary violations.

patient.name: Professional Boundaries
patient.admitDate: During a prolonged admission a patient offers gifts, requests the nurse's personal phone number, and asks to be social-media friends. The nurse must maintain a therapeutic, professional relationship.

labs.r: Helps the patient, not the nurse.

labs.v: Only if it benefits the patient.

nursesNote: Over a prolonged admission, a patient offers the nurse an expensive gift, asks for the nurse's personal phone number to stay in touch, and requests to be social-media friends. The nurse-patient relationship is therapeutic: patient-centered, goal-directed, professional, and time-limited, ending at discharge. The nurse keeps interactions focused on the patient's care goals, limits self-disclosure to what benefits the patient, and recognizes warning signs of over-involvement (off-duty contact, secret-keeping, accepting money or expensive gifts) as well as under-involvement (neglect, disinterest). The nurse politely declines the gift and personal contact, does not friend, follow, post about, or photograph patients on social media, and never enters a romantic or sexual relationship with a patient. If objectivity becomes compromised, the nurse consults the supervisor, considers transferring care, and documents appropriately.
providerOrders: Keep the relationship patient-centered, professional, and goal-directed
Decline money or expensive gifts and personal/social-media contact
Limit self-disclosure to what benefits the patient
Recognize over- or under-involvement and consult the supervisor
Protect confidentiality; never form a romantic relationship with a patient

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What defines a therapeutic professional relationship?

opts.text: The nurse-patient relationship is patient-centered, goal-directed, and time-limited
Excessive personal self-disclosure shifts focus away from the patient
Giving the patient your personal phone number to text you anytime is appropriate
Spending off-duty time socializing with a patient is a warning sign of over-involvement
Brief self-disclosure may be therapeutic if it benefits the patient
A romantic relationship with an assigned patient is acceptable if both consent
Accepting money or expensive gifts from patients is a boundary violation
Nurses should not friend, follow, or post about patients on social media
Keeping a patient's secret that affects their care is appropriate
Under-involvement (neglect, disinterest) is also a boundary problem
Recognizing personal feelings and consulting/transferring care can protect boundaries
Photographing patients for personal social media is fine with a verbal okay

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to a patient seeking a personal relationship

opts.text: Recognize the boundary issue and your own feelings
Kindly clarify the professional, patient-centered nature of the relationship
Redirect interactions back to the patient's care goals
Decline personal contact, gifts, or social-media connection per policy
Consult the supervisor or transfer care if objectivity is compromised
Keeping interactions patient-centered and goal-directed
Politely declining a patient's offer of money
Giving the patient your personal cell number to text anytime
Friending the patient on social media
Brief self-disclosure that refocuses on the patient's goals
Beginning a romantic relationship with an assigned patient
Consulting your manager about feelings of over-involvement
Posting a photo with the patient on your personal page

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence the response to a gift + personal-contact request
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Sincerely acknowledge the patient's kind gesture
Explain professional boundaries and the policy on gifts
Politely decline the expensive gift and personal contact
Redirect to the therapeutic relationship and care goals
Document and consult the supervisor or policy as needed
Maintaining a patient-centered, goal-directed relationship
Politely declining money or expensive gifts
Recognizing over-involvement and consulting the supervisor
Keeping interactions professional on and off social media
Giving a patient your personal phone number for after discharge
Starting a romantic relationship with an assigned patient
Posting about or photographing a patient online
Spending personal off-duty time socializing with a patient
